cards.grammarNote:
**agua** is feminine but takes **el** in the singular (starts with stressed 'a')
Adjectives still agree feminine: **el agua fría** (cold water) · Plural: **las aguas**

**pan** is masculine: **el pan** · **Quisiera** is the imperfect subjunctive of **querer** used as a polite 'I would like' — softer than **quiero** (I want)
Common in restaurants throughout the Spanish-speaking world

**arroz** is masculine: **el arroz** · Comes from Arabic **al-ruzz** — one of many Arabic food words in Spanish
**Me gusta** (it pleases me) is how Spanish expresses liking: the thing liked is the grammatical subject, so singular items use **gusta** and plural items use **gustan**: **me gustan las verduras**
cards.phrase: Me gusta el arroz con verduras.

cards.grammarNote:
**leche** is feminine: **la leche** · **¿Quieres?** is the tú (informal) form of **querer** — an e→ie stem-changing verb
Full present: **quiero, quieres, quiere, queremos, queréis, quieren**

**huevo** is masculine: **el huevo**, plural **los huevos** · **desayuno** doubles as both the noun (breakfast) and the yo-form verb **desayunar** (to have breakfast)
**Como** (I eat) is the yo form of **comer** — a regular -er verb: como, comes, come, comemos, coméis, comen
cards.englishPhrase: I eat two eggs for breakfast.
cards.phrase: Como dos huevos en el desayuno.

cards.grammarNote:
**queso** is masculine: **el queso** · **sándwich** is an English borrowing fully adopted into Spanish, written with an accent: **el sándwich**
**tiene** is the él/ella form of **tener** (to have): tengo, tienes, tiene, tenemos, tenéis, tienen

**pollo** is masculine: **el pollo** · **está delicioso** uses **estar** rather than **ser** because food quality is a temporary, subjective condition
Compare: **La manzana es verde** (ser — inherent color) vs **La sopa está caliente** (estar — current temperature)

**carne** is feminine: **la carne** · The subject pronoun **Él** (he) is optional in Spanish — it is included here for emphasis or clarity
Note: **él** (he) carries an accent to distinguish it from **el** (the)

**pescado** (cooked/caught fish, food) vs **pez** (live fish in water) — the same distinction as English 'fish (to eat)' vs 'fish (the animal)'
**pescado** is masculine: **el pescado**

**churros** are always masculine plural: **los churros** · Traditionally served with thick hot **chocolate** for dipping — a classic Spanish breakfast
**Quiero** (I want) is the yo form of the stem-changing verb **querer** (e→ie): quiero, quieres, quiere…
cards.englishPhrase: I want churros with hot chocolate.

cards.grammarNote:
**paella** is feminine: **la paella** · A Valencian dish — **valenciana** is a feminine adjective agreeing with **paella** (adjectives must match the gender and number of the noun)
**mariscos** (seafood) is typically masculine plural: **los mariscos**
The word **paella** comes from the Latin **patella** (pan) via the Valencian language
cards.englishPhrase: Valencia paella has rice and seafood.
cards.phrase: La paella valenciana tiene arroz y mariscos.

cards.grammarNote:
**tapas** are feminine plural: **las tapas** · From **tapar** (to cover) — legend says a slice of bread or ham was placed on top of a drink to keep flies out
**pedir** (to order/ask for) is a stem-changing e→i verb: **pido, pides, pide, pedimos, pedís, piden** — used for ordering food and requesting things
**Vamos a + infinitive** is the 'let's' construction: **vamos a pedir** = let's order

**cerdo** is masculine: **el cerdo** · Also means 'pig' (the animal) — context makes the meaning clear
In Spain, **jamón** (cured ham from pork) is central to culinary culture; **jamón ibérico** and **jamón serrano** are famous regional varieties

**fruta** is feminine: **la fruta** (uncountable, as a category) · Individual pieces: **una fruta** (a piece of fruit)
**todos los días** = every day — literally 'all the days'; **día** is masculine despite ending in -a: **el día**

**manzana** is feminine: **la manzana** · Also means 'city block' in Latin America — context distinguishes the two meanings
**al día** = per day / a day — **al** is the contraction of **a + el**
cards.englishPhrase: An apple a day is healthy.
cards.phrase: Una manzana al día es saludable.

cards.grammarNote:
**plátano** is masculine: **el plátano** · Regional variation: in many Latin American countries the word **banano** or **banana** is preferred for the sweet fruit; **plátano** may specifically mean the larger cooking plantain
**maduros** (ripe) — adjective agrees masculine plural with **plátanos**: maduro → maduros

**naranja** is feminine: **la naranja** · Etymology: from Sanskrit **nāranga** → Persian → Arabic **nāranja** → Spanish **naranja** — a classic Arabic food borrowing
In Latin America **jugo** = juice; in Spain **zumo** is more common: **zumo de naranja**
Diminutive of affection: **naranjita** (little orange / dear orange)

**verdura** is feminine: **la verdura** · Comes from **verde** (green) — originally meant 'green things'
Also common: **vegetal** or **hortaliza** for individual vegetables · **¡Come las verduras!** is a tú imperative (informal command): the tú imperative of -er verbs uses the él/ella form: **come** (from comer)

**papa** is feminine: **la papa** (used throughout Latin America) · In Spain: **patata** — same vegetable, different word
Both come from Quechua **papa** — the potato is native to the Andes
**puré de papas** (mashed potatoes) — **puré** is masculine: **el puré**

**tomate** is masculine: **el tomate** · From Nahuatl **tomatl** — a word borrowed into Spanish during the colonial period
**ensalada** is feminine: **la ensalada** · **tiene** = has (él/ella form of **tener**)

**cebolla** is feminine: **la cebolla** · **No me gustan** — note the verb is plural (**gustan**) because the subject is **las cebollas** (plural)
With **gustar**, the verb agrees with what is liked, not the person: **me gusta el café** but **me gustan las cebollas**

**ensalada** is feminine: **la ensalada** · From **sal** (salt) — originally 'salted greens'
**Voy a tomar** uses **ir a + infinitive** for the near future: 'I'm going to have…'
**tomar** literally means 'to take' but is widely used for eating/drinking: **tomar un café**, **tomar una ensalada**

**sopa** is feminine: **la sopa** · **está caliente** uses **estar** (not **ser**) for the temporary state of being hot — food temperature is always expressed with **estar**
Diminutive: **sopita** (a nice little soup / soup served affectionately)

**sándwich** is an English borrowing: **el sándwich** — note the written accent on the first syllable following Spanish stress rules
In Mexico a long sandwich roll is called a **torta**; in Cuba **medianoche** or **pan con** varieties are common — regional vocabulary varies widely
**Hice** is the irregular preterite (past tense) yo form of **hacer** (to make/do): hice, hiciste, hizo, hicimos, hicisteis, hicieron
cards.englishPhrase: I made a sandwich for lunch.
cards.phrase: Hice un sándwich para el almuerzo.

cards.grammarNote:
**pastel** is masculine: **el pastel** · In Mexico and Central America **pastel** = cake; in Spain **tarta** is more common for layer cakes
**pastel** also means 'pastel (color)' and 'pie' depending on context — a versatile word!

**galleta** is feminine: **la galleta** · **galleta** covers both cookies (sweet) and crackers (savory) — context or a modifier clarifies: **galleta salada** (cracker), **galleta dulce** (sweet cookie)
**estas galletas** — **estas** is the feminine plural demonstrative: esta → estas (this/these)

**chocolate** is masculine: **el chocolate** · From Nahuatl **xocolātl** (bitter water) — brought to Europe from Mesoamerica in the 16th century
**Me encanta** = I love it (literally 'it enchants me') — **encantar** works exactly like **gustar**: the thing loved is the grammatical subject

**azúcar** from Arabic **al-sukkar** (itself from Sanskrit **śarkarā**) — one of the clearest Arabic food etymologies in Spanish
**azúcar** is unusual: it is used with both **el** and **la** — **el azúcar** is more common in Spain, **la azúcar** in parts of Latin America
**sin** = without — used before a noun with no article: **sin azúcar** (no sugar), **sin sal** (no salt), **sin leche** (no milk)

**sal** is feminine: **la sal** · **¿Me pasas la sal?** is an indirect request using the present tense — more natural in Spanish than the English-style 'could you pass?'
The indirect object pronoun **me** (to me) is placed before the conjugated verb

**pimienta** is feminine: **la pimienta** · Refers to ground black/white pepper (the spice)
Distinguish from **pimiento** (masculine: **el pimiento**) = bell pepper or chile pepper — the vegetable
From Latin **pigmentum** (pigment, substance) via medieval cooking vocabulary

**mantequilla** is feminine: **la mantequilla** · From **manteca** (lard/fat) + diminutive **-illa** — literally 'little fat'
In Spain **mantequilla** = butter; **manteca** = lard · In Argentina **manteca** can mean butter
**Pongo** is the irregular yo form of **poner** (to put): pongo, pones, pone, ponemos, ponéis, ponen
cards.englishPhrase: I put butter on my bread.

cards.grammarNote:
**aceite** is masculine: **el aceite** · From Arabic **az-zayt** (the olive oil) — another key Arabic culinary borrowing
Spain is one of the world's largest olive oil producers; **aceite de oliva virgen extra** (extra virgin olive oil) is a staple
**oliva** = the olive (fruit/tree); **aceituna** is the more common word for the olive you eat

**café** is masculine: **el café** · From Arabic **qahwa** → Turkish **kahve** → Spanish **café**
Diminutive of affection: **cafecito** — very common in Latin America, used to warmly offer coffee: **¿Un cafecito?**
**por la mañana** = in the morning; also: **por la tarde** (in the afternoon), **por la noche** (in the evening/at night)
cards.englishPhrase: I like coffee in the morning.
cards.phrase: Me gusta el café por la mañana.

cards.grammarNote:
**té** is masculine: **el té** · Written with an accent to distinguish it from **te** (the indirect object pronoun 'you/to you')
**¿Quieres un té?** — note the indefinite article **un** before **té**; in English we often omit the article ('some tea'), but Spanish commonly uses **un/una**

**vino** is masculine: **el vino** · Color terms for wine differ from English: **vino tinto** (red wine — literally 'dyed/tinted wine'), **vino blanco** (white wine), **vino rosado** (rosé)
**copa** = wine glass (stemmed glass); **vaso** = regular drinking glass — use **copa** for wine, **vaso** for water/juice
cards.englishPhrase: A glass of red wine, please.
cards.phrase: Una copa de vino tinto, por favor.

cards.grammarNote:
**cerveza** is feminine: **la cerveza** · From Latin **cervesia** — a Gaulish/Celtic word that entered Latin
In Spain, a small beer on tap is called a **caña**; a larger glass is a **jarra** · In Latin America ordering **una chela** (informal) is common in Mexico

**jugo** is masculine: **el jugo** · Used throughout Latin America; in Spain the preferred word is **zumo**: **zumo de naranja**
Diminutive: **jugito** — used affectionately, especially when offering juice to children: **¿Quieres un jugito?**

**desayuno** is masculine: **el desayuno** · From **des-** (un-) + **ayuno** (fast) — 'breaking the fast,' just like English 'breakfast'
**Desayuno** (I have breakfast) — the verb **desayunar** is also regular: desayuno, desayunas, desayuna…
**a las siete** = at seven — times use **a la/las** (at one o'clock: **a la una**; other hours: **a las dos/tres**…)

**almuerzo** is masculine: **el almuerzo** · **almorzar** (to have lunch) is an o→ue stem-changing verb: **almuerzo, almuerzas, almuerza, almorzamos, almorzáis, almuerzan**
In Spain, **la comida** (the meal) typically refers to lunch — the main meal of the day, eaten around 2–3 pm

**cena** is feminine: **la cena** · **cenar** = to have dinner (regular -ar verb)
**está lista** — **estar** for the current state (readiness) and **lista** agrees feminine with **la cena**
In Spain dinner (**la cena**) is typically light and eaten late, around 9–10 pm

**merienda** is feminine: **la merienda** · The mid-afternoon snack, typically around 5–6 pm — particularly important for children in Spain and Latin America
**como una merienda** — here **como** = I eat (from **comer**), not 'like/as'
**por la tarde** = in the afternoon — compare **por la mañana** (morning) / **por la noche** (evening)
cards.englishPhrase: I have a snack in the afternoon.
cards.phrase: Como una merienda por la tarde.

cards.grammarNote:
**comida** is feminine: **la comida** · From **comer** (to eat) — literally 'what is eaten'
**comida** has multiple meanings: food in general, a meal, or specifically lunch (in Spain)
**Esta es una comida deliciosa** — note **deliciosa** agrees feminine with **comida**

**receta** is feminine: **la receta** · Doubles as both 'recipe' and 'prescription' (medical) — context makes it clear
**¿Tienes una buena receta?** — **buena** (feminine) agrees with **receta** · Adjectives that end in -o/-a change for gender: **bueno/buena**, **delicioso/deliciosa**
cards.englishPhrase: Do you have a good recipe?

cards.grammarNote:
**sabor** is masculine: **el sabor** · Related to **saber** (to know, but also 'to taste'): **saber a** = to taste like: **Sabe a chocolate** (It tastes like chocolate)
Basic taste vocabulary: **dulce** (sweet), **amargo** (bitter), **ácido/agrio** (sour), **salado** (salty), **picante** (spicy), **umami** (umami)

**ingrediente** is masculine: **el ingrediente**, plural **los ingredientes** · Nouns ending in -nte are usually masculine
**necesitas** is the tú form of **necesitar** (to need) — a regular -ar verb: necesito, necesitas, necesita, necesitamos, necesitáis, necesitan

**menú** is masculine: **el menú** · Written with an accent on the **ú** — borrowed from French **menu**
**¿Puedo ver…?** = Can I see…? — **poder** (can/to be able) is an o→ue stem-changing verb: **puedo, puedes, puede, podemos, podéis, pueden**
Also common in restaurants: **la carta** = the menu (the physical card listing dishes)

**restaurante** is masculine: **el restaurante** · From French **restaurant** ('restoring') — the original French restaurants advertised 'restorative broths'
**está bien** uses **estar** for a current quality (how things are going) rather than **es bueno** (ser) which would state an inherent permanent characteristic

**cocina** is feminine: **la cocina** · **cocina** is a versatile word: it means 'kitchen' (the room), 'stove/cooker' (the appliance), and is also the él/ella/usted form of **cocinar** (to cook)
**está limpia** — **estar** for condition; **limpia** (clean) agrees feminine with **cocina**

**horno** is masculine: **el horno** · **al horno** = baked/roasted (in the oven): **pollo al horno** (roast chicken), **papas al horno** (baked potatoes)
From Latin **furnus** — the Spanish word underwent sound change f→h at the start of words (also: **farina** → **harina**, **filium** → **hijo**)

**estufa** is feminine: **la estufa** · In Latin America **estufa** = kitchen stove/cooker; in Spain the stove/hob is usually **la cocina** or **los fogones**
**Cocínalo** is a command (tú imperative) with a direct object pronoun attached: **cocina + lo** (cook + it) — pronouns attach to the end of affirmative commands

**cuchillo** is masculine: **el cuchillo** · Related to **cuchilla** (blade) and **cuchara** (spoon) — all from Latin **cochlearium** (ladle/spoon), which spawned a family of eating utensil words
**afilado** (sharp) agrees masculine with **cuchillo** — feminine would be **afilada**

**tenedor** is masculine: **el tenedor** · From **tener** (to hold/have) — literally 'the holder'
**¿Me da un tenedor?** uses the usted form **da** (gives) — polite register appropriate for asking a waiter
Compare tú form: **¿Me das un tenedor?** (more informal)

**cuchara** is feminine: **la cuchara** · Diminutive **cucharita** = teaspoon; **cucharada** = a spoonful (a common unit in recipes)
**cucharón** (augmentative) = a large ladle/serving spoon — Spanish uses suffixes to indicate size: -ito/-ita (small), -ón/-ona (large)

**plato** is masculine: **el plato** · **plato** also means 'dish' (a prepared food item): **el plato del día** (dish of the day), **un plato de pasta** (a plate of pasta)
**está sucio** — **estar** for condition; **sucio** (dirty) agrees masculine with **plato**

**tazón** is masculine: **el tazón** · Augmentative of **taza** (cup) — **-ón** suffix makes it bigger: **taza** (cup) → **tazón** (large cup / bowl)
**Llena** is the tú imperative of **llenar** (to fill) — regular -ar verb imperative: llena, compra, habla…
**Llena el tazón de sopa** — **de** here means 'with' (filled with)

**vaso** is masculine: **el vaso** · **vaso** = everyday drinking glass; **copa** = stemmed glass (wine, champagne)
**un vaso de agua** — the construction **un/una [container] de [contents]** is the standard way to order drinks: **un vaso de agua**, **una taza de café**, **una copa de vino**
cards.phrase: Un vaso de agua, por favor.

cards.grammarNote:
**botella** is feminine: **la botella** · From Old French **boteille** (bottle)
**una botella de vino** — same [container] de [contents] pattern · Useful in restaurants: **una botella de agua mineral** (a bottle of mineral water), **con gas** (sparkling) or **sin gas** (still)
cards.phrase: Una botella de vino, por favor.

cards.grammarNote:
**mesa** is feminine: **la mesa** · From Latin **mensa** (table, also altar)
**Nuestra mesa está lista** — **nuestra** (our) is a possessive adjective agreeing feminine with **mesa**: nuestro/nuestra, vuestro/vuestra
**está lista** — **lista** (ready) agrees feminine here; note **lista** also means 'list' and 'clever'

**camarero** (masculine) / **camarera** (feminine) = waiter/waitress · Primarily used in Spain
Regional alternatives: **mesero/mesera** (Mexico, Colombia), **mozo/moza** (Argentina, Peru), **garzón** (Chile)
**la cuenta** (the bill/check) is feminine: remember to say **¿Me trae la cuenta?** (Can you bring me the bill?)

Use **tener** (to have) for hunger, thirst, and other physical states:
**tener hambre** (hungry) · **tener sed** (thirsty) · **tener frío/calor** (cold/hot) · **tener miedo** (afraid) · **tener sueño** (sleepy)
These are expressed with **tener**, not **estar**

**sed** (thirst) is feminine: **la sed** — though the article is rarely used in the **tener sed** construction
**el hambre** and **la sed** are both feminine nouns used in the same pattern: **tener hambre/sed** (to be hungry/thirsty — literally 'to have hunger/thirst')
Note the gender surprise: **el hambre** is actually feminine — it uses **el** only to avoid the vowel clash **la hambre → el hambre**

**comer** is a regular -er verb: **como, comes, come, comemos, coméis, comen**
**¡Vamos a comer!** — **vamos a + infinitive** = let's [do something] (also functions as near future: 'we are going to eat')
**comer** can be used as a noun with the article: **la comida** or informally **el comer**: **El comer sano es importante**

**beber** is a regular -er verb: **bebo, bebes, bebe, bebemos, bebéis, beben**
Synonym: **tomar** (to take/drink) — widely used for beverages: **tomar agua**, **tomar café**; **beber** is slightly more formal/literary
**algo de beber** = something to drink — **algo de + infinitive** = something to [do]
cards.englishPhrase: Would you like something to drink?

cards.grammarNote:
**cocinar** is a regular -ar verb: **cocino, cocinas, cocina, cocinamos, cocináis, cocinan**
The person who cooks is **el/la cocinero/a** (cook/chef); a more skilled chef: **el/la chef** (borrowed from French)
**cocina muy bien** — adverbs like **bien** (well) and **mal** (badly) don't change form

**hornear** (to bake) comes from **horno** (oven) — a regular -ar verb
Alternative: **cocer al horno** (to cook in the oven) is also common
**los domingos** = on Sundays (every Sunday) — using the plural definite article with a day of the week expresses habitual action

**freír** is an e→i stem-changing verb (like **pedir**): **frío, fríes, fríe, freímos, freís, fríen**
Note the written accent on **fríe** (command/present) to distinguish it from **frie** (uncommon)
**sofreír** = to sauté (lightly fry) — a useful cooking verb

**asar** is a regular -ar verb meaning to roast or grill over fire
**Asa el pollo** — **asa** is the tú imperative form
**durante** = for (a duration of time): **durante una hora** (for one hour) — use **durante** for duration, not **por** when timing a cooking step
cards.englishPhrase: Roast the chicken for one hour.
cards.phrase: Asa el pollo durante una hora.

cards.grammarNote:
**picar** means to chop, mince, or dice — and also 'to sting/bite' and 'to be spicy'
**picar** is a regular -ar verb but has a spelling change in the preterite yo form: **piqué** (not picé) — c→qu before e to preserve the hard /k/ sound
**Pica las cebollas** — **pica** is the tú imperative

**rebanar** (to slice) is a regular -ar verb · From **rebana** (a slice of bread)
Alternatives: **cortar** (to cut, more general), **laminar** (to slice thinly)
**Rebana el pan** — **rebana** is the tú imperative of a regular -ar verb

**mezclar** is a regular -ar verb · **mezcla** = the mix/mixture (noun, feminine)
**Mezcla todos los ingredientes** — **mezcla** is both the tú imperative and the él/ella present tense form (context distinguishes them)
**todos los ingredientes** — **todos** (all/every, masculine plural) agrees with the masculine plural **ingredientes**

**revolver** is an o→ue stem-changing verb: **revuelvo, revuelves, revuelve, revolvemos, revolvéis, revuelven**
Note: **revolver** also means 'to rummage through' or refers to the firearm (revólver) — an accent on the noun distinguishes them
**Revuelve la sopa** — **revuelve** is both the tú imperative and the él/ella form

**batir** is a regular -ir verb: **bato, bates, bate, batimos, batís, baten**
**batir** also means to beat, to whip, and to defeat — the same word covers whisking eggs, whipping cream, and beating an opponent
**Bate los huevos** — **bate** is the tú imperative of **batir**

**verter** is an e→ie stem-changing verb: **vierto, viertes, vierte, vertemos, vertéis, vierten**
Alternative: **echar** is the everyday word for pouring/adding liquids in cooking: **échale agua** (pour in some water)
**Vierte el agua en el vaso** — note **el agua** (feminine noun with masculine article for vowel harmony)
cards.englishPhrase: Pour the water into the glass.
cards.phrase: Vierte el agua en el vaso.

cards.grammarNote:
**servir** is an e→i stem-changing verb (same pattern as **pedir**): **sirvo, sirves, sirve, servimos, servís, sirven**
**sirvió** is the él/ella preterite (past) form — note the e→i stem change carries into the preterite for -ir verbs: sirvió, sirvieron
**servir** also means 'to be useful': **¿Para qué sirve?** (What is it good for?)

**alimentar** is a regular -ar verb · Related noun: **el alimento** (food/nourishment), **la alimentación** (diet/nutrition)
**alimenta al bebé** — the **a** before **bebé** is the 'personal a' required before specific human direct objects: **Veo al médico** (I see the doctor), **Llamo a mi madre** (I call my mother)
**al** = **a + el** (mandatory contraction)

**cenar** is a regular -ar verb · **Cenamos** is both the present tense (we dine) and the preterite (we dined) — context determines tense
Here it is preterite: **Cenamos** = we dined · The nosotros present and preterite of -ar verbs are identical: **hablamos** = we speak / we spoke
**restaurante agradable** — adjective **agradable** (pleasant) doesn't change for gender (same ending for masculine and feminine)
cards.englishPhrase: We dined at a nice restaurant.

cards.grammarNote:
**masticar** is a regular -ar verb with a spelling change in the preterite yo form: **mastiqué** (c→qu before e)
**Mastica la comida despacio** — **mastica** is the tú imperative
**despacio** (slowly) is an adverb — adverbs are invariable (no gender/number agreement): mastica despacio, come despacio

**tragar** is a regular -ar verb with a spelling change in the preterite yo form: **tragué** (g→gu before e)
**Traga la pastilla** — **traga** is the tú imperative
**pastilla** is feminine: **la pastilla** · Note **agua** in the phrase: **el agua** (feminine noun, masculine article) — see the 'water' card

**sazonar** is a regular -ar verb · Related: **el sazón** (seasoning/flavor), **la sazón** in the Caribbean means 'the knack for cooking' or a spice blend
**Sazona con sal y pimienta** — **sazona** is the tú imperative
**y** (and) becomes **e** before words starting with i- or hi-: **sal e hierbas** (salt and herbs)

**fresco** is an adjective that changes for gender and number: **fresco** (m. sg.), **fresca** (f. sg.), **frescos** (m. pl.), **frescas** (f. pl.)
**El pan está fresco** — **estar** for current condition (freshness is temporary)
**fresco** also means 'cool' (weather): **Está fresco afuera** (It's cool outside)

**maduro** (ripe) changes for gender and number: **maduro/madura/maduros/maduras**
**maduro** also means 'mature' for people: **una persona madura** (a mature person)
**Los tomates están maduros** — **estar** because ripeness is a temporary condition

**cocido** is the past participle of **cocer** (to cook/boil), used here as an adjective
As an adjective it must agree: **cocido/cocida/cocidos/cocidas** — here **cocido** agrees masculine with **el pollo**
**¿Está cocido el pollo?** — **estar** for the current state; the subject (**el pollo**) comes after the verb (common in questions and when adding emphasis)

Use **estar** for food and drink temperatures — they are temporary states
**La sopa está caliente** · **El café está frío**
Compare with **ser** for inherent qualities: **El chile es picante** (chili is always spicy)
cards.englishPhrase: Be careful, the soup is hot.

cards.grammarNote:
**frío** (cold) changes for gender: **frío/fría/fríos/frías**
**Mi café está frío** — **estar** for the temporary condition of being cold
**frío** is also the noun: **el frío** (the cold); **tener frío** = to be cold (of a person): **Tengo frío** (I'm cold) — same **tener** pattern as hunger and thirst

**dulce** doesn't change for gender (same ending for masculine and feminine): **el café dulce**, **la limonada dulce**
**Este pastel está muy dulce** — **estar** for the current subjective taste experience
**dulce** is also a noun: **el dulce** = candy/sweet treat, especially in Mexico and Central America · **los dulces** = candies

**agrio** (sour) changes for gender: **agrio/agria** · Also common: **ácido/ácida** (acidic/sour)
**Este limón está agrio** — **estar** for the current taste condition
Note: **limón** in Latin America usually means lime (the small green citrus); **limón amarillo** or **lima** may be used for the yellow lemon

**amargo** (bitter) changes for gender: **amargo/amarga/amargos/amargas**
**Este café está amargo** — **estar** for taste as a current condition
**amargo** is also used figuratively: **una experiencia amarga** (a bitter experience), **palabras amargas** (bitter words)

**picante** doesn't change for gender: **el chile picante**, **la salsa picante**
**suele ser picante** — **soler + infinitive** means 'to usually do' (habitual action): **suelo comer** (I usually eat)
**ser** (not **estar**) because spiciness is an inherent characteristic of Mexican food — compare with **La sopa está caliente** (temporary state) vs **El chile es picante** (permanent trait)
cards.phrase: La comida mexicana suele ser picante.

cards.grammarNote:
**tierno** (tender) changes for gender: **tierno/tierna/tiernos/tiernas**
**La carne está tierna** — **estar** for the current condition of the meat after cooking
**tierno** also means 'young/fresh' (for bread, cheese) and 'tender/affectionate' for people: **un niño tierno** (a sweet/tender child)

**delicioso** changes for gender: **delicioso/deliciosa/deliciosos/deliciosas**
**¡Esto está delicioso!** — **esto** (this) is a neuter demonstrative used when the item hasn't been specified or is abstract · **está** (not **es**) because deliciousness is a subjective sensory experience
Alternatives for 'delicious': **riquísimo** (super tasty), **buenísimo** (really good), **¡qué rico!** (how tasty!)

**sabroso** (tasty) changes for gender: **sabroso/sabrosa** · From **sabor** (flavor)
**La sopa está muy sabrosa** — **sabrosa** agrees feminine with **sopa**; **estar** for the subjective taste quality
Synonyms: **rico/rica** (common in everyday speech: **¡Qué rico!**), **bueno/buena** (**¡Está buenísimo!**)

**soso** (bland/tasteless) changes for gender: **soso/sosa**
**El arroz está soso** — **estar** for the current condition; **soso** agrees masculine with **arroz**
**soso** also means 'dull/boring' for people or situations: **una persona sosa** (a boring person) · Antonym: **sabroso** (tasty), **sazonado** (well-seasoned)

**saludable** doesn't change for gender: **el desayuno saludable**, **la comida saludable**
**La fruta es saludable** — **ser** (not estar) because healthiness is an inherent characteristic, not a temporary state
Related words: **la salud** (health), **sano/sana** (healthy, as an adjective for people and food — more colloquial than **saludable**)

**insalubre** doesn't change for gender: **el hábito insalubre**, **la comida insalubre**
**La comida rápida es insalubre** — **ser** because unhealthiness is an inherent trait of fast food
More common in everyday speech: **no es sano/a** or **es malo/a para la salud** (it's bad for health)
**comida rápida** = fast food — a calque (loan translation) of the English term

**vegetariano/vegetariana** — the adjective agrees with the person: a woman says **Soy vegetariana**
**Soy** (I am, from **ser**) is used for identity and defining characteristics — being vegetarian is a permanent choice/identity, so **ser** is correct
Note: **¿Hay opciones vegetarianas?** (Are there vegetarian options?) — useful restaurant phrase

**vegano/vegana** agrees with the person: **Ella es vegana** (she is vegan)
**Soy vegano/vegana** uses **ser** for identity — same pattern as **vegetariano**
Useful phrases: **sin lácteos** (dairy-free), **sin huevo** (egg-free), **sin productos animales** (no animal products)

**congelado** is the past participle of **congelar** (to freeze), used here as an adjective
It agrees: **congelado/congelada/congelados/congeladas** — here **congelada** agrees feminine with **comida**
**Saca** is the tú imperative of **sacar** (to take out) — spelling change in preterite: **saqué** (c→qu before e)

**orgánico** changes for gender: **orgánico/orgánica/orgánicos/orgánicas** — here **orgánicas** agrees feminine plural with **verduras**
**Prefiero** is the yo form of **preferir** (to prefer) — an e→ie stem-changing verb: **prefiero, prefieres, prefiere, preferimos, preferís, prefieren**
In Latin America the term **ecológico/a** (organic/ecological) is also widely used

**casero** (homemade/homestyle) comes from **casa** (house) — literally 'house-made'
It changes for gender: **casero/casera/caseros/caseras** — here **casero** agrees masculine with **pan**
**Me encanta** = I love it — **encantar** (to love/enchant) works like **gustar**: singular subject → **encanta**, plural → **encantan**: **me encantan las galletas caseras**

**diario** (daily) can be an adjective or adverb · As an adjective it changes for gender: **el periódico diario** (the daily newspaper)
**a diario** = daily/on a daily basis — a fixed adverbial phrase
Note the difference: **todos los días** (every day — more common in speech) vs **a diario** (daily — slightly more formal)

**casi** is an invariable adverb (no gender/number change)
**El almuerzo está casi listo** — **listo** (ready) agrees masculine with **almuerzo** here; note **listo** also means 'clever' when used with **ser**: **Eres muy listo** (You're very clever)
**casi** + number expressions: **casi las dos** (almost two o'clock), **casi un kilo** (almost a kilo)

**demasiado** agrees in gender and number when used as an adjective before a noun
**demasiada sal** (too much salt) — feminine to match **sal** (feminine)
As an adverb before adjectives/other adverbs it is invariable: **demasiado picante** (too spicy), **demasiado rápido** (too fast)
**Hay** = there is/there are — invariable form of **haber** used to state existence
